author: @LaurieGilbert-Dowd-ys2mp
text: That’s not a plasma tv/ where are you 3 circular colored glass that sits between the other like magnifying glass about 4 inches in diameter one glass component is green, one red and I forget yellow or blue. And there is a clear liquid under birth the glass, like mineral oil or something. If it was a plasma tv you would lay it down like that . I drove for Bekins trade shows and we had 103inch plazma tv/ we couldn’t even turn it sideways to get it up the service elevator at the mall of America. We set up a Christmas living room display with the tv as the main attraction. 70,000.00$ for that tv, it was 8’x5’. But yeah there is liquid in them so like a fridge you cannot lay them down . Thank you for the video/ but that is not a plazma tv

Personally I've just been having fun with the light refractor sheets and making cool effects in photography projects. But idk what to do with all the transformers and capacitors and stuff

author: @savetheaspies
text: Question. Do you know if printer scanners use optically pure glass? I have been getting into dumpster diving / scrapping and I'm wondering what a fair price is for that kind of glass. I always try to sell dirt cheap as its free to me... yet in this situation there is a notable quality/price difference. Plain glass $5-10 is fair... Optically pure... 30-50 is still dirt cheap... yet more fair to my pocket lol.

author: @thehumbledabbler
text: You explain quite well how to "figure out" how to take something apart lol. Something that really did used to be common sense. Something I figured out in my own at 7 yrs old while disassembling a keyboard (musical) that I found in the dumpster. I assumed everyone on the planet knew that to get something apart, you look at it, see what's preventing it from coming loose, then remove whatever it is, and keep going like that till it's apart.... Until I asked my son to swap out the air filter on on the lawnmower one day.. 30 minutes later I'm standing there in bewilderment as he was trying to figure out how this spongy piece just magically moves through the metal case into its position, paying no mind to the single bolt that had to be removed first... Maybe those video games really DO rot their minds..

author: @inkman6964
text: I have just got a plasma tv from 3 doors up and crazy heavy I’m surprised you never put a multimeter on the screen as I did thinking with it being plasma the sun gives off plasma it works as a solar cell the 22 connectors on the bottom of the screen give 7.6v in total and haven’t tested the side ones yet but loving your videos as everything you are doing is what I’m looking into but for different reasons

author: @grokstone
text:
Hi Robert, I just want to say that I am always anticipating the moments before watching your videos. You are very professional yet your ability to draw us in with your exploration of each of these interesting projects is like an addiction. It is without a doubt, the most entertaining and educational videos which ends up being very productive for those who wish to follow your lead. Actually, as we look onto Mars as our next major endeavor for the future, I imagine you continuing your series especially the reclamation of so many components from so many manufactured components. On Mars, there will be only reclamation, repair and ingenuity to keep any colonists alive during there stay there.
Maybe they should bring copies of your video series or just subscribe from Mars, its only a 20 minute delay!
Anyhow, thanks once again for the wonderful inspiration.
All the best,
Mark

author: @richardteychenne3950
text: Loved the video Rob, although I might suggest a safety reminder at the beginning. If anyone is silly enough to do a plug in test to see if it works and then go straight into dismantling. If the PUS has any faults which prevent the high voltage capacitors from quickly discharging then the retained voltages can be at lethal potentials for some time. Any of the younger viewers will only be used to modern low voltage circuits and not the Tesla ( that's his holiness Nicola not the Baywatch one) coil voltages of real old school TVs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the post mate - hope you are well

author: @yougeo
text: i think they used a lot of magnesium for those structural parts. cheap and good strength to weight ratio. getting harder and harder to find good large  transformers and flyback coils since less and less things use high voltage now. good to save that stuff and the heat sinks. that aluminum plate is really one massive heatsink i suspect. That tv probably heated a whole rooom.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - yeah it got hot and magnesium makes sense now i think of it - cheers mate - good pointer and appreciated

author: @foley2k2
text: Need IGBTs? You can get 23 of them out of the Prius drivetrain (the inverter) - those are liquid cooled and sealed in gel. Junked cars are a good source since nobody's ever going to fix the old ones due to high battery cost.

author: @zacharycawthorne-nugent4988
text: Loved this video! It got me thinking, are there any resources you’d recommend for a beginner to get to know what the parts are called and what they do. I’m a complete beginner but really interested! Thanks for the great video

replies.author: @andrewcooke8850
replies.text:
Zachary Cawthorne-Nugent you could read many books but the best way to learn is by doing. Find an old item, open it up and search the part numbers (where available) of the components you are curious about and you will find their identity. Use this terminology to search for info about them and their uses. 
Also, download free schematics of DIY projects and have a go at building them. 
Google and YouTube are an awesome resource.
